## DIFFUSION-BASED VOICE CONVERSION WITH FAST MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD SAMPLING SCHEME

## **Vadim Popov, Ivan Vovk, Vladimir Gogoryan,**

Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab, Moscow, Russia

_{_ vadim.popov,vovk.ivan,gogoryan.vladimir _}_ @huawei.com

## **Tasnima Sadekova, Mikhail Kudinov & Jiansheng Wei**

Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab, Moscow, Russia

_{_ sadekova.tasnima,kudinov.mikhail,weijiansheng _}_ @huawei.com

## ABSTRACT

Voice conversion is a common speech synthesis task which can be solved in different ways depending on a particular real-world scenario. The most challenging one often referred to as one-shot many-to-many voice conversion consists in copying target voice from only one reference utterance in the most general case when both source and target speakers do not belong to the training dataset. We present a scalable high-quality solution based on diffusion probabilistic modeling and demonstrate its superior quality compared to state-of-the-art one-shot voice conversion approaches. Moreover, focusing on real-time applications, we investigate general principles which can make diffusion models faster while keeping synthesis quality at a high level. As a result, we develop a novel Stochastic Differential Equations solver suitable for various diffusion model types and generative tasks as shown through empirical studies and justify it by theoretical analysis.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Voice conversion (VC) is the task of copying the target speaker’s voice while preserving the linguistic content of the utterance pronounced by the source speaker. Practical VC applications often require a model which is able to operate in one-shot mode (i.e. when only one reference utterance is provided to copy the target speaker’s voice) for any source and target speakers. Such models are usually referred to as one-shot many-to-many models (or sometimes zero-shot many-to-many models, or just any-to-any VC models). It is challenging to build such a model since it should be able to adapt to a new unseen voice having only one spoken utterance pronounced with it, so it was not until recently that successful one-shot VC solutions started to appear.

Conventional one-shot VC models are designed as autoencoders whose latent space ideally contains only the linguistic content of the encoded utterance while target voice identity information (usually taking shape of speaker embedding) is fed to the decoder as conditioning. Whereas in the pioneering AutoVC model (Qian et al., 2019) only speaker embedding from the pre-trained speaker verification network was used as conditioning, several other models improved on AutoVC enriching conditioning with phonetic features such as pitch and loudness (Qian et al., 2020; Nercessian, 2020), or training voice conversion and speaker embedding networks jointly (Chou & Lee, 2019). Also, several papers (Lin et al., 2021; Ishihara & Saito, 2020; Liu et al., 2021b) made use of attention mechanism to better fuse specific features of the reference utterance into the source utterance thus improving the decoder performance. Apart from providing the decoder with sufficiently rich information, one of the main problems autoencoder VC models face is to disentangle source speaker identity from speech content in the encoder. Some models (Qian et al., 2019; 2020; Nercessian, 2020) solve this problem by introducing an information bottleneck. Among other popular solutions of the disentanglement problem one can mention applying vector quantization technique to the content information (Wu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021), utilizing features of Variational AutoEncoders (Luong & Tran, 2021; Saito et al., 2018; Chou & Lee, 2019), introducing instance normalization layers (Chou & Lee, 2019; Chen et al., 2021b), and using Phonetic Posteriorgrams (PPGs) (Nercessian, 2020; Liu et al., 2021b).

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

The model we propose in this paper solves the disentanglement problem by employing the encoder predicting “average voice”: it is trained to transform mel features corresponding to each phoneme into mel features corresponding to this phoneme averaged across a large multi-speaker dataset. As for decoder, in our VC model, it is designed as a part of a Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DPM) since this class of generative models has shown very good results in speech-related tasks like raw waveform generation (Chen et al., 2021a; Kong et al., 2021) and mel feature generation (Popov et al., 2021; Jeong et al., 2021). However, this decoder choice poses a problem of slow inference because DPM forward pass scheme is iterative and to obtain high-quality results it is typically necessary to run it for hundreds of iterations (Ho et al., 2020; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021). Addressing this issue, we develop a novel inference scheme that significantly reduces the number of iterations sufficient to produce samples of decent quality and does not require model re-training. Although several attempts have been recently made to reduce the number of DPM inference steps (Song et al., 2021a; San-Roman et al., 2021; Watson et al., 2021; Kong & Ping, 2021; Chen et al., 2021a), most of them apply to some particular types of DPMs. In contrast, our approach generalizes to all popular kinds of DPMs and has a strong connection with likelihood maximization.

This paper has the following structure: in Section 2 we present a one-shot many-to-many VC model and describe DPM it relies on; Section 3 introduces a novel DPM sampling scheme and establishes its connection with likelihood maximization; the experiments regarding voice conversion task as well as those demonstrating the benefits of the proposed sampling scheme are described in Section 4; we conclude in Section 5.

## 2 VOICE CONVERSION DIFFUSION MODEL

As with many other VC models, the one we propose belongs to the family of autoencoders. In fact, any conditional DPM with data-dependent prior (i.e. terminal distribution of forward diffusion) can be seen as such: forward diffusion gradually adding Gaussian noise to data can be regarded as encoder while reverse diffusion trying to remove this noise acts as a decoder. DPMs are trained to minimize the distance (expressed in different terms for different model types) between the trajectories of forward and reverse diffusion processes thus, speaking from the perspective of autoencoders, minimizing reconstruction error. Data-dependent priors have been proposed by Popov et al. (2021) and Lee et al. (2021), and we follow the former paper due to the flexibility of the continuous DPM framework used there. Our approach is summarized in Figure 1.

## 2.1 ENCODER

We choose average phoneme-level mel features as speaker-independent speech representation. To train the encoder to convert input mel-spectrograms into those of “average voice”, we take three steps: (i) first, we apply Montreal Forced Aligner (McAuliffe et al., 2017) to a large-scale multi-speaker LibriTTS dataset (Zen et al., 2019) to align speech frames with phonemes; (ii) next, we obtain average mel features for each particular phoneme by aggregating its mel features across the whole LibriTTS dataset; (iii) the encoder is then trained to minimize mean square error between output mel-spectrograms and ground truth “average voice” mel-spectrograms (i.e. input mel-spectrograms where each phoneme mel feature is replaced with the average one calculated on the previous step).

The encoder has exactly the same Transformer-based architecture used in Grad-TTS (Popov et al., 2021) except that its inputs are mel features rather than character or phoneme embeddings. Note that unlike Grad-TTS the encoder is trained separately from the decoder described in the next section.

The described above DPM was introduced by Popov et al. (2021) for text-to-speech task and we adapt it for our purposes. We put _X_[¯] = _ϕ_ ( _X_ 0) where _ϕ_ is the encoder, i.e. _X_[¯] is the “average voice” mel-spectrogram which we want to transform into that of the target voice. We condition the decoder _sθ_ = _sθ_ ( _X_[ˆ] _t, X, g_[¯] _t_ ( _Y_ ) _, t_ ) on some trainable function _gt_ ( _Y_ ) to provide it with information about the target speaker ( _Y_ stands for forward trajectories of the target mel-spectrogram at inference and the ones of the training mel-spectrogram at training). This function is a neural network trained jointly with the decoder. We experimented with three input types for this network:
- _d-only_ – the input is the speaker embedding extracted from the target mel-spectrogram _Y_ 0 with the pre-trained speaker verification network employed in (Jia et al., 2018);

- _wodyn_ – in addition, the noisy target mel-spectrogram _Yt_ is used as input;

- _whole_ – in addition, the whole dynamics of the target mel-spectrogram under forward diffusion _{Ys|s_ = 0 _._ 5 _/_ 15 _,_ 1 _._ 5 _/_ 15 _, ..,_ 14 _._ 5 _/_ 15 _}_ is used as input.

The decoder architecture is based on U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) and is the same as in GradTTS but with four times more channels to better capture the whole range of human voices. The speaker conditioning network _gt_ ( _Y_ ) is composed of 2D convolutions and MLPs and described in detail in Appendix H. Its output is 128-dimensional vector which is broadcast-concatenated to the concatenation of _X_[ˆ] _t_ and _X_[¯] as additional 128 channels.

## 2.3 RELATED VC MODELS

To the best of our knowledge, there exist two diffusion-based voice conversion models: VoiceGrad (Kameoka et al., 2020) and DiffSVC (Liu et al., 2021a). The one we propose differs from them in several important aspects. First, neither of the mentioned papers considers a one-shot many-tomany voice conversion scenario. Next, these models take no less than 100 reverse diffusion steps at inference while we pay special attention to reducing the number of iterations (see Section 3) achieving good quality with as few as 6 iterations. Furthermore, VoiceGrad performs voice conversion by running Langevin dynamics starting from the source mel-spectrogram, thus implicitly assuming that forward diffusion trajectories starting from the mel-spectrogram we want to synthesize are likely to pass through the neighborhood of the source mel-spectrogram on their way to Gaussian noise. Such an assumption allowing to have only one network instead of encoder-decoder architecture is too strong and hardly holds for real voices. Finally, DiffSVC performs singing voice conversion and relies on PPGs as speaker-independent speech representation.

## 3 MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD SDE SOLVER

In this section, we develop a fixed-step first-order reverse SDE solver that maximizes the loglikelihood of sample paths of the forward diffusion. This solver differs from general-purpose Euler-Maruyama SDE solver (Kloeden & Platen, 1992) by infinitesimally small values which can however become significant when we sample from diffusion model using a few iterations.
Consider the following forward and reverse SDEs defined in Euclidean space R _[n]_ for _t ∈_ [0 _,_ 1]:

where _[−→] W_ is a forward Wiener process (i.e. its forward increments _[−→] Wt − W[−→] s_ are independent of _[−→] Ws_ for _t > s_ ) and _[←−] W_ is a backward Wiener process (i.e. backward increments _[←−] Ws − W[←−] t_ are independent of _[←−] Wt_ for _s < t_ ). Following Song et al. (2021c) we will call DPM (6) Variance Preserving (VP). For simplicity we will derive maximum likelihood solver for this particular type of diffusion models. The equation (1) underlying VC diffusion model described in Section 2 can be transformed into the equation (6-F) by a constant shift and we will call such diffusion models Mean Reverting Variance Preserving (MR-VP). VP model analysis carried out in this section can be easily extended (see Appendices D, E and F) to MR-VP model as well as to other common diffusion model types such as sub-VP and VE described by Song et al. (2021c).

Note that certain Lipschitz constraints should be satisfied by coefficients of SDEs (6) to guarantee existence of strong solutions (Liptser & Shiryaev, 1978), and throughout this section we assume these conditions are satisfied as well as those from (Anderson, 1982) which guarantee that paths _X_[ˆ] generated by the reverse SDE (6-R) for the optimal _θ[∗]_ equal forward SDE (6-F) paths _X_ in distribution.

The generative procedure of a VP DPM consists in solving the reverse SDE (6-R) backwards in time starting from _X_[ˆ] 1 _∼N_ (0 _,_ I). Common Euler-Maruyama solver introduces discretization error (Kloeden & Platen, 1992) which may harm sample quality when the number of iterations is small. At the same time, it is possible to design unbiased (Henry-Labordere et al., 2017) or even exact (Beskos` & Roberts, 2005) numerical solvers for some particular SDE types. The Theorem 1 shows that in the case of diffusion models we can make use of the forward diffusion (6-F) and propose a reverse SDE solver which is better than the general-purpose Euler-Maruyama one in terms of likelihood.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We trained two groups of models: _Diff-VCTK_ models on VCTK (Yamagishi et al., 2019) dataset containing 109 speakers (9 speakers were held out for testing purposes) and _Diff-LibriTTS_ models on LibriTTS (Zen et al., 2019) containing approximately 1100 speakers (10 speakers were held out). For every model both encoder and decoder were trained on the same dataset. Training hyperparameters, implementation and data processing details can be found in Appendix I. For mel-spectrogram inversion, we used the pre-trained universal HiFi-GAN vocoder (Kong et al., 2020) operating at 22 _._ 05kHz. All subjective human evaluation was carried out on Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT) with Master assessors to ensure the reliability of the obtained Mean Opinion Scores (MOS). In all AMT tests we considered unseen-to-unseen conversion with 25 unseen (for both _Diff-VCTK_ and _Diff-LibriTTS_ ) speakers: 9 VCTK speakers, 10 LibriTTS speakers and 6 internal speakers. For VCTK source speakers we also ensured that source phrases were unseen during training. We place other details of listening AMT tests in Appendix J. A small subset of speech samples used in them is available at our demo page https://diffvc-fast-ml-solver.github.io which we encourage to visit. The code will soon be published at https://github.com/huawei-noah/ Speech-Backbones.

## 4.1 SPEAKER CONDITIONING ANALYSIS

For each dataset we trained three models – one for each input type for the speaker conditioning network _gt_ ( _Y_ ) (see Section 2.2). Although these input types had much influence neither on speaker similarity nor on speech naturalness, we did two experiments to choose the best models (one for each training dataset) in terms of speaker similarity for further comparison with baseline systems. We compared voice conversion results (produced by _ML-30_ sampling scheme) on 92 source-target pairs. AMT workers were asked which of three models (if any) sounded most similar to the target speaker and which of them (if any) sounded least similar. For _Diff-VCTK_ and _Diff-LibriTTS_ models each conversion pair was evaluated 4 and 5 times respectively. Table 1 demonstrates that for both _Diff-VCTK_ and _Diff-LibriTTS_ the best option is _wodyn_ , i.e. to condition the decoder at time _t_ on the speaker embedding together with the noisy target mel-spectrogram _Yt_ . Conditioning on _Yt_ allows making use of diffusion-specific information of how the noisy target sounds whereas embedding from the pre-trained speaker verification network contains information only about the clean target. Taking these results into consideration, we used _Diff-VCTK-wodyn_ and _Diff-LibriTTS-wodyn_ in the remaining experiments.

## 4.2 ANY-TO-ANY VOICE CONVERSION

We chose four recently proposed VC models capable of one-shot many-to-many synthesis as the baselines:
- _AGAIN-VC_ (Chen et al., 2021b), an improved version of a conventional autoencoder AdaINVC solving the disentanglement problem by means of instance normalization;
- _FragmentVC_ (Lin et al., 2021), an attention-based model relying on wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) to obtain speech content from the source utterance;

- _VQMIVC_ (Wang et al., 2021), state-of-the-art approach among those employing vector quantization techniques;
- _BNE-PPG-VC_ (Liu et al., 2021b), an improved variant of PPG-based VC models combining a bottleneck feature extractor obtained from a phoneme recognizer with a seq2seq-based synthesis module.

As shown in (Kim et al., 2021), PPG-based VC models provide high voice conversion quality competitive even with that of the state-of-the-art VC models taking text transcription corresponding to the source utterance as input. Therefore, we can consider _BNE-PPG-VC_ a state-of-the-art model in our setting.

Baseline voice conversion results were produced by the pre-trained VC models provided in official GitHub repositories. Since only _BNE-PPG-VC_ has the model pre-trained on a large-scale dataset (namely, LibriTTS + VCTK), we did two subjective human evaluation tests: the first one comparing _Diff-VCTK_ with _AGAIN-VC_ , _FragmentVC_ and _VQMIVC_ trained on VCTK and the second one comparing _Diff-LibriTTS_ with _BNE-PPG-VC_ . The results of these tests are given in Tables 2 and 3 respectively. Speech naturalness and speaker similarity were assessed separately. AMT workers evaluated voice conversion quality on 350 source-target pairs on 5-point scale. In the first test, each pair was assessed 6 times on average both in speech naturalness and speaker similarity evaluation; as for the second one, each pair was assessed 8 and 9 times on average in speech naturalness and speaker similarity evaluation correspondingly. No less than 41 unique assessors took part in each test.

Table 2 demonstrates that our model performs significantly better than the baselines both in terms of naturalness and speaker similarity even when 6 reverse diffusion iterations are used. Despite working almost equally well on VCTK speakers, the best baseline _VQMIVC_ shows poor performance on other speakers perhaps because of not being able to generalize to different domains with lower recording quality. Although _Diff-VCTK_ performance also degrades on non-VCTK speakers, it achieves good speaker similarity of MOS 3 _._ 6 on VCTK ones when _ML-30_ sampling scheme is used and only slightly worse MOS 3 _._ 5 when 5x less iterations are used at inference.

Table 3 contains human evaluation results of _Diff-LibriTTS_ for four sampling schemes: _ML-30_ with 30 reverse SDE solver steps and _ML-6_ , _EM-6_ and _PF-6_ with 6 steps of reverse diffusion. The three schemes taking 6 steps achieved real-time factor (RTF) around 0 _._ 1 on GPU (i.e. inference was 10 times faster than real time) while the one taking 30 steps had RTF around 0 _._ 5. The proposed model _Diff-LibriTTS-ML-30_ and the baseline _BNE-PPG-VC_ show the same performance on the VCTK test set in terms of speech naturalness the latter being slightly better in terms of speaker similarity which can perhaps be explained by the fact that _BNE-PPG-VC_ was trained on the union of VCTK and LibriTTS whereas our model was trained only on LibriTTS. As for the whole test set containing unseen LibriTTS and internal speakers also, _Diff-LibriTTS-ML-30_ outperforms _BNE-PPG-VC_ model achieving MOS 4 _._ 0 and 3 _._ 4 in terms of speech naturalness and speaker similarity respectively. Due to employing PPG extractor trained on a large-scale ASR dataset LibriSpeech (Panayotov et al., 2015), _BNE-PPG-VC_ has fewer mispronunciation issues than our model but synthesized speech suffers from more sonic artifacts. This observation makes us believe that incorporating PPG features in the proposed diffusion VC framework is a promising direction for future research.
Table 3 also demonstrates the benefits of the proposed maximum likelihood sampling scheme over other sampling methods for a small number of inference steps: only _ML-N_ scheme allows us to

## 4.3 MAXIMUM LIKELIHOOD SAMPLING

Figure 2: CIFAR-10 images randomly sampled from VP DPM by running 10 reverse diffusion steps with the following schemes (from left to right): “euler-maruyama”, “probability flow”, “maximum likelihood ( _τ_ = 0 _._ 5)”, “maximum likelihood ( _τ_ = 1 _._ 0)”.

To show that the maximum likelihood sampling scheme proposed in Section 3 generalizes to different tasks and DPM types, we took the models trained by Song et al. (2021c) on CIFAR-10 image generation task and compared our method with other sampling schemes described in that paper in terms of Fr´echet Inception Distance (FID).

The main difficulty in applying maximum likelihood SDE solver is estimating data-dependent term E[Tr (Var ( _X_ 0 _|Xt_ ))] in _σt,h[∗]_[.][Although in the current experiments we just set this term to zero, we] can think of two possible ways to estimate it: (i) approximate Var ( _X_ 0 _|Xt_ ) with Var ( _X_[ˆ] 0 _|X_[ˆ] _t_ = _Xt_ ): sample noisy data _Xt_ , solve reverse SDE with sufficiently small step size starting from terminal condition _X_[ˆ] _t_ = _Xt_ several times, and calculate sample variance of the resulting solutions at initial points _X_[ˆ] 0; (ii) use the formula (58) from Appendix C to calculate Var ( _X_ 0 _|Xt_ ) assuming that _X_ 0 is distributed normally with mean and variance equal to sample mean and sample variance computed on the training dataset. Experimenting with these techniques and exploring new ones seems to be an interesting future research direction.

Another important practical consideration is that the proposed scheme is proven to be optimal only for score matching networks trained till optimality. Therefore, in the experiments whose results are reported in Table 4 we apply maximum likelihood sampling scheme only when _t ≤ τ_ while using standard Euler-Maruyama solver for _t > τ_ for some hyperparameter _τ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1]. Such a modification relies on the assumption that score matching network is closer to being optimal for smaller noise.

Table 4 shows that despite likelihood and FID are two metrics that do not perfectly correlate (Song et al., 2021b), in most cases our maximum likelihood SDE solver performs best in terms of FID. Also, it is worth mentioning that although _τ_ = 1 is always rather a good choice, tuning this hyperparameter can lead to even better performance. One can find randomly chosen generated images for various sampling methods in Figure 2.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, the novel one-shot many-to-many voice conversion model has been presented. Its encoder design and powerful diffusion-based decoder made it possible to achieve good results both in terms of speaker similarity and speech naturalness even on out-of-domain unseen speakers. Subjective human evaluation verified that the proposed model delivers scalable VC solution with competitive performance. Furthermore, aiming at fast synthesis, we have developed and theoretically justified the novel sampling scheme. The main idea behind it is to modify the general-purpose Euler-Maruyama SDE solver so as to maximize the likelihood of discrete sample paths of the forward diffusion. Due to the proposed sampling scheme, our VC model is capable of high-quality voice conversion with as few as 6 reverse diffusion steps. Moreover, experiments on the image generation task show that all known diffusion model types can benefit from the proposed SDE solver.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a technically ambitious and well-motivated contribution to the field of one-shot many-to-many voice conversion, proposing a diffusion-based generative framework augmented with a novel fast sampling scheme grounded in maximum likelihood principles. The writing is generally clear, and the structure follows a logical flow typical of high-quality conference submissions, with a solid review of prior work in voice conversion and diffusion models. The core idea—leveraging an "average voice" encoder to promote content-speaker disentanglement—is conceptually sound and aligns well with current research trends aiming to improve representation learning in generative models. However, a significant shortcoming lies in the abrupt truncation of the technical description: the model architecture section cuts off mid-sentence, leaving critical details about the encoder’s Transformer-based design and the full system architecture unspecified, which undermines the reproducibility and credibility of the method. Additionally, while the introduction promises both a novel VC model and a general-purpose accelerated solver for diffusion models, the balance between these two contributions is unclear at this stage; it remains to be seen whether the sampling scheme is sufficiently evaluated across multiple diffusion types to justify its claimed generality. There is also a risk of overclaiming—asserting superior quality over state-of-the-art methods without yet presenting quantitative or qualitative evidence in the provided text. For a top-tier conference like ICLR, greater precision in articulating technical innovations and a more complete exposition of the model design are essential to support the strong claims being made.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of ground truth for encoder training
comments.quote: the encoder is then trained to minimize mean square error between output mel-spectrograms and ground truth “average voice” mel-spectrograms (i.e. input mel-spectrograms where each phoneme mel feature is replaced with the average one calculated on the previous step).
comments.explanation: The phrase 'input mel-spectrograms where each phoneme mel feature is replaced with the average one' could be misinterpreted as being performed dynamically during training. However, the method description clarifies that average mel features are precomputed across LibriTTS using MFA alignments in a preprocessing step and used as a fixed lookup table. Thus, for each frame aligned to a phoneme, the corresponding precomputed average mel vector is substituted. While the procedure is logically sound and reproducible given the context, the phrasing may confuse readers about whether the replacement is online or offline. A clearer rewording would be: 'input mel-spectrograms with each frame's features replaced by the precomputed average for its corresponding phoneme'.

comments.title: Misleading implication about disentanglement via average voice encoder
comments.quote: The model we propose in this paper solves the disentanglement problem by employing the encoder predicting “average voice”: it is trained to transform mel features corresponding to each phoneme into mel features corresponding to this phoneme averaged across a large multi-speaker dataset.
comments.explanation: The claim that the encoder 'solves the disentanglement problem' is overstated. While mapping to an average voice representation discards speaker-specific information, it does not guarantee robust content-speaker disentanglement in the latent space, especially with respect to fine linguistic details like prosody or co-articulation. True disentanglement typically requires invariance of content representations to speaker identity under varied test conditions. This method enforces speaker neutrality via template replacement, which is a design choice rather than a learned disentangled representation. The approach is empirically validated downstream, but the theoretical claim exaggerates the mechanism's capabilities. A more accurate characterization would be that it 'enforces speaker neutrality' rather than 'solves disentanglement'.

This is inconsistent with established definitions of probability flow in diffusion models (e.g., Song et al., 2021c). In standard formulations, probability flow ODE is obtained by setting the noise coefficient to zero (i.e. σ̂_t,h = 0), but the drift is fully determined by the score and β_t—the coefficient multiplying the score term, ω̂_t,h, should be non-zero and time-dependent. Here, the solver is defined as using ω̂_t,h = 0, which would result in no score contribution at all—meaning the update becomes simply X̂_{t−h} = X̂_t − 0.5 X̂_t h = X̂_t (1 − 0.5h), an exponential decay toward zero. This is clearly not probability flow sampling. I checked whether this is an OCR error: perhaps κ̂_t,h = −0.5 was meant to be ω̂_t,h = −β_t or something similar. But κ̂_t,h is supposed to multiply X̂_t, and setting it to −0.5 would only make sense for a specific β_t. The correct form for the MR-VP model would involve ω̂_t,h = β_t / (2(1 - e^{-∫β_s ds})) or similar. Given that the paper contrasts ML-N with PF-N and shows PF-N achieving better FID than Euler-Maruyama in Table 4, the solver used in experiments must be properly implemented, but the text as written describes an incorrect version. This is a critical discrepancy between the reported method and the definition.

comments.title: Missing Justification for κ̂_t,h = −0.5 in PF-N
comments.explanation: Even if we take the definition at face value, the choice of κ̂_t,h = −0.5 is arbitrary and unjustified. The reverse SDE (6-R) has drift coefficient −1/2 β_t X_t, so if this κ̂_t,h were approximating that, it should depend on β_t and h. Setting it to a constant −0.5 only makes sense if β_t h ≈ 1 for all t, which is not typical. Moreover, in the absence of a score term (ω̂_t,h=0), this would correspond to a degenerate process unrelated to data generation. Given that probability flow sampling should retain the score-based drift, omitting ω̂_t,h entirely contradicts the concept. The authors are careful about theoretical consistency elsewhere (e.g., Theorem 1, connection to likelihood), so this appears to be either a misstatement or a notation error. The fact that PF-N shows reasonable FID in Table 4 suggests the actual implementation differed from the text. Without clarification, this undermines trust in the method description.

comments.title: Ambiguity in ML Solver Approximation Affects Reproducibility
comments.quote: assuming this variance to be rather small because of strong conditioning on _gt_ ( _Y_ ) and just used ˆ _σt,h_ = _σt−h,t_
comments.explanation: The authors state they omit the conditional variance term in σ*_t,h due to estimation difficulty, which is reasonable and acknowledged. However, instead of using a default such as √(β_t h) (as in Euler-Maruyama), they use an undefined ‘σ_{t−h,t}’. This is a key implementation detail: if the noise level is too high or too low, it can degrade sample quality or cause instability. In the context of fast sampling (N=6), the noise schedule approximation is critical. The paper claims improvements over Euler-Maruyama and PF, but without a clear specification of what σ̂_t,h is set to, the reproduction of the ML-N method is impossible. The only clue is in Appendix D (mentioned in context) which may define it, but within the provided sections, this remains underspecified. Given the central role of the sampling scheme, this is a significant gap in methodological transparency.

comments.title: Ambiguous treatment of conditional variance approximation in ML-N sampling
comments.quote: assuming this variance to be rather small because of strong conditioning on _gt_ ( _Y_ ) and just used ˆ _σt,h_ = _σt−h,t_ calling this sampling method _ML-N_ ( _N_ = 1 _/h_ is the number of SDE solver steps).
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the passage claims to use the maximum likelihood (ML) solver while explicitly omitting a key term—E[Tr(Var(X₀|Xₜ))]—which appears in the definition of the optimal parameter σₜ,ₕ∗ from Theorem 1. The full expression for σₜ,ₕ∗ involves correcting √βₜh by a term that depends on this conditional variance. By setting this term to zero, the practical implementation deviates from the theoretically optimal solver. The authors justify this with the argument that strong conditioning makes the conditional distribution of X₀ given Xₜ nearly deterministic (i.e., low variance). This is plausible in tasks like mel-spectrogram inversion where conditioning is highly informative. However, the text does not quantify or empirically validate this assumption, nor does it clarify how close to 'constant' the posterior X₀|Xₜ actually is. While this approximation may be reasonable, calling the resulting method 'maximum likelihood'—especially in contrast to Euler-Maruyama and probability flow—could mislead readers into thinking the full theoretical benefits of Theorem 1(i) are achieved in practice. The gap between theory and implementation is acknowledged in later text (e.g., 'we skip this term'), but the naming ('ML-N') overstates the connection to maximum likelihood. A more accurate name might be 'approximate ML' or 'ML-inspired'.

comments.title: Confusing omission in FID evaluation context undermines method clarity
comments.quote: we apply maximum likelihood sampling scheme only when _t ≤ τ_ while using standard Euler-Maruyama solver for _t > τ_ for some hyperparameter _τ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1].
comments.explanation: At first glance, this adaptive switching strategy seems like a reasonable heuristic: use the more sophisticated ML solver when the score network is more reliable (i.e., at low noise, small t), and default to EM at high noise. However, the passage fails to specify which τ values were actually used in Table 4, nor does it report ablations for different τ (only mentions that τ = 1 'is always rather a good choice'). More importantly, the text states that results are shown 'in Table 4' but Table 4 is not included in the provided context. This makes it impossible to assess whether the ML solver improves FID and under what conditions. Without seeing the actual numbers or knowing the τ that produced them, the claim in Section 5 that 'our maximum likelihood SDE solver performs best in terms of FID' lacks support. While the overall approach is sound, the description is underspecified for reproducibility. The authors are careful to note the theoretical reliance on optimal score matching, but fail to report critical experimental choices (τ values, evaluation metrics with breakdown), making it hard to judge the practical significance of the method beyond voice conversion.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for solver parameter in theoretical vs practical definition
comments.quote: just used ˆ _σt,h_ = _σt−h,t_ calling this sampling method _ML-N_
comments.explanation: The paper defines the general solver update as involving ˆ_σ̂ₜ,ₕ √h ξₜ. Later, in the definition of _ML-N_, it says they set ˆ_σ̂ₜ,ₕ = σₜ₋ₕ,ₜ. But σₜ₋ₕ,ₜ is a diffusion coefficient (likely O(1)), so unless σₜ₋ₕ,ₜ is meant to scale with √h, this would make the noise term O(1) rather than O(√h), breaking the standard Euler-Maruyama scaling and potentially leading to numerical instability. Checking the context: in the forward SDE (Eq. 6-F), the noise coefficient is √βₜ dWₜ, so increments scale with √h. In the Euler-Maruyama solver, ˆ_σ̂ₜ,ₕ = √(βₜ h), which is consistent. The notation σₜ₋ₕ,ₜ is ambiguous. It likely refers to the marginal standard deviation between t and t−h in the forward process, which for linear βₜ is proportional to √(∫ₜ₋ₕ^t βₛ ds) ≈ √(βₜ h) for small h. Thus, the intended meaning of σₜ₋ₕ,ₜ is probably √(βₜ h), aligning with the EM solver’s ˆ_σ̂ₜ,ₕ. However, the notation σₜ₋ₕ,ₜ suggests a finite difference or fixed variance, not scaled by h. This could confuse a reader unfamiliar with the marginal statistics of VP-SDEs. The authors should explicitly define σₜ₋ₕ,ₜ as √(βₜ h) or as the marginal standard deviation to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Overstatement of generality in conclusion without supporting evidence
comments.quote: Moreover, experiments on the image generation task show that all known diffusion model types can benefit from the proposed SDE solver.
comments.explanation: This concluding claim asserts that 'all known diffusion model types' benefit from the proposed solver. However, the only external experiment described is on CIFAR-10 using a VP DPM (Song et al., 2021c), and the generalization to sub-VP and VE is mentioned only in appendices (D, E, F), which are not provided. While the method may indeed generalize, as suggested by the authors' analytical extensions, the phrase 'all known diffusion model types' is too strong. No results are shown for VE (variance exploding), sub-VP, or discrete-time models. Furthermore, the claim relies on Appendix claims that are not verifiable in the current context. A more accurate statement would be: 'the proposed solver can be extended to other common diffusion types (e.g., sub-VP, VE), and initial experiments on VP models show improvements in FID', which reflects the actual evidence. As written, the conclusion overreaches the results.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to unstated numerical degradation values
comments.quote: use as few as _N_ = 6 iterations with acceptable quality degradation of MOS 0 _._ 2 and 0 _._ 1 in terms of naturalness and speaker similarity respectively
comments.explanation: The passage claims a 'quality degradation of MOS 0.2 and 0.1' when using N=6 vs higher step counts. However, no baseline is specified: degradation relative to what? The most natural baseline would be the same model with more steps (e.g., Diff-VCTK-ML-30). Looking at Table 2, Diff-VCTK-ML-6 and ML-30 have identical mean naturalness (3.73) on the VCTK test, and only a 0.10 difference in speaker similarity (3.47 vs 3.57), which matches the claim. But in Table 3, Diff-LibriTTS-ML-6 vs ML-30 shows a 0.12 difference in naturalness (3.80 vs 4.02) and 0.12 in similarity (3.27 vs 3.39)—not 0.2 and 0.1. The degradation values are plausible for a specific model (VCTK-ML), but presenting them as universal values across models is misleading. The text does not clarify which model these numbers refer to, potentially leading readers to assume they are general. Given that the surrounding discussion focuses on the VC models, the numbers likely come from Table 2. But without explicit reference, the claim is ambiguous. This is a minor issue because the values are reasonable and contextually supported, but precision would improve clarity.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper is well-structured and technically ambitious, effectively positioning itself within the current state-of-the-art landscape of diffusion-based speech synthesis. The authors provide a clear, logical progression from the limitations of existing autoencoder-based voice conversion (VC) to their proposed diffusion framework, effectively highlighting the trade-off between synthesis quality and inference speed. The narrative flow is cohesive, and the introduction does a commendable job of grounding the "disentanglement" problem—a major pain point in the field—within the context of a new "average voice" encoding strategy. However, the most significant challenge for the paper will be the technical rigor of the proposed "novel Stochastic Differential Equations solver." While the authors promise a generalizable, likelihood-based approach, the writing would benefit from a more explicit bridge between the theoretical justification of this acceleration and the specific empirical trade-offs involved in voice conversion. Furthermore, ensuring that the claims of "superior quality" remain robust against potential artifacts introduced by the accelerated sampling scheme will be critical for the paper's overall impact.

comments.title: Missing definition and formatting of the reverse SDE solver update rule
comments.quote: As for sampling, we considered the following class of reverse SDE solvers: [Equation missing/mangled]
comments.explanation: The OCR failed to capture the formal update equation for the reverse SDE solver, leaving only fragmented text. As this solver is the primary technical contribution (intended to reduce iteration count), its absence renders the methodology underspecified and impossible to reproduce. This consolidation covers the fragmentation found in paragraph 31, 41, and 45.

comments.explanation: The notation $Y$ is heavily overloaded, simultaneously representing the reference signal, the noisy sample, and the entire diffusion trajectory. Additionally, the conditioning input is referred to inconsistently as $g_t(Y)$ and $\bar{g}_t(Y)$, and the appearance of the variable $X$ within the decoder conditioning lacks distinction from the latent 'average voice' representation. This ambiguity obscures the actual architectural requirements and input dimensionality of the conditioning network.

comments.title: Lack of clarity regarding SDE drift and 'average voice' representation
comments.quote: d X_t = -\frac{1}{2} \beta_t X_t d t + \sqrt{\beta_t} d \vec W_t
comments.explanation: The relationship between the diffusion process and the 'average voice' prior $X_{\bar{}}$ is technically underspecified. The SDE formulation uses non-standard notation for $X_{\bar{}}$ and lacks a clear definition of its role in the drift relative to the target data $X_t$. Without a precise link between the diffusion prior and the specific shift/drift terms, the mathematical foundation of the model is fragmented.

comments.title: Underspecified hyperparameter in Maximum Likelihood Solver

comments.explanation: The methodology section fails to define the parameter $\tau$ used in the results (Table 4). Since this parameter governs the behavior of the 'Maximum Likelihood' solver, its omission makes the reported results unverifiable and the specific solver implementation irreproducible.

comments.title: Mathematical Notation Error in Forward SDE Definition
comments.quote: where $t \in [0, 1]$, $\vec W$ and $\overleftarrow W$ are two independent Wiener processes in $\mathbb{R}^n$, $\beta_t$ is non-negative function referred to as noise schedule , $s_\theta$ is the score function with parameters $\theta$ and $\bar X$ is $n$-dimensional vector.
comments.explanation: The OCR likely misinterpreted the notation for the Wiener processes. In the context of the subsequent equations (1) and (2) and standard stochastic calculus literature (as referenced by the authors), these are typically denoted as $W_t$ rather than using the over-arrow notation which represents a directed Wiener process. While the authors use $\vec W$ in the text, equation (1) uses $\text{d}W_t$ and (2) uses $\text{d}\bar W_t$. Furthermore, $\bar X$ is mentioned as an $n$-dimensional vector, but in Equation (1), it appears as $\bar X$, which usually refers to a specific signal or target, not just an arbitrary vector. This is a recurring minor OCR/transcription error regarding the notation for Wiener processes and symbols that should ideally be consistent.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Speaker Conditioning Network notation
comments.quote: $g_t ( Y ) \dots \text{where } Y = \{Y_t\}_{t\in [0, 1]} \text{ is the whole forward diffusion trajectory starting at } Y_0$
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the authors define $Y$ as the target mel-spectrogram at inference, but then state it is the 'whole forward diffusion trajectory'. This is conceptually inconsistent: a trajectory is a set of spectrograms (at different $t$), but the conditioning network $g_t(Y)$ typically operates on specific features or embeddings extracted from $Y_0$. If it truly takes the *whole* trajectory as input, the computational cost would be prohibitive. Based on later descriptions (like 'd-only' or 'wodyn'), the notation $Y$ is overloaded, simultaneously representing the reference signal, the noisy sample, and the trajectory. This could mislead a reader about the actual dimensionality and input requirements of the $g_t$ network.

comments.title: Potential sign error in Diffusion SDE drift
comments.quote: $d X_t = -\frac{1}{2} \beta_t X_t d t + \sqrt{\beta_t} d \vec W_t$
comments.explanation: Equation (1) as OCR'd is missing elements or symbols that typically appear in the standard VP-SDE formulation (often involving terms like $\bar X$ or specific drift functions). Given the context of Section 2.2, the code expects the forward SDE to reduce to the data-dependent prior. The current notation indicates a standard Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, but the text later says the model solves for $\bar X$ (denoting an 'average voice' representation). The relationship between the diffusion process and the shift $\bar X$ needs clearer definition to ensure that the recovery of $X_0$ is mathematically sound for all $t$.

comments.title: Ambiguous variable notation in decoder conditioning
comments.quote: We condition the decoder $s_\theta = s_\theta ( \hat{X}_t, X, \bar{g}_t ( Y ) , t )$
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the conditioning input is listed as $\bar{g}_t(Y)$. However, in the preceding sentence, this function is referred to as $g_t(Y)$. Checking the surrounding context, there is no definition for an overline notation $\bar{g}_t$ versus $g_t$. It appears to be an OCR misread or a recurring typo for $g_t(Y)$, which is defined later in the same paragraph as the 'speaker conditioning network'. While a reader can infer the intent, the inconsistent notation is confusing.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for conditioning variable
comments.quote: We condition the decoder $s_\theta = s_\theta ( \hat{X}_t, X, \bar{g}_t ( Y ) , t )$
comments.explanation: The decoder conditioning function is written as $\bar{g}_t(Y)$, yet the text mentions 'speaker conditioning network $g_t(Y)$'. Furthermore, the variable $X$ appears as a conditioning argument. In previous definitions, $X$ is usually the target data or trajectory, and $X_{\bar{t}}$ (or similar variants) are being used for the 'average voice' latents. The appearance of $X$ here, without clear distinction from the latent 'average voice' representation $X_{\bar{}}$ or the noisy sample $\hat{X}_t$, creates potential ambiguity about the architecture.

comments.title: Notation conflict between SDE indices and variables
comments.quote: Consider the following forward and reverse SDEs defined in Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^n$ for $t \in [0, 1]:$ ... $X_{\bar{}}$ is $n$-dimensional vector.
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, $X_{\bar{}}$ is defined as the 'average voice' mel-spectrogram. However, in Section 3, the equations (6-F/R) introduce $X$ variables, and the subscripts are used for time ($X_t$). The notation $X_{\bar{}}$ (with a bar, or possibly an OCR error for $X_{\bar{t}}$ or similar) persists in the text but is not consistently indexed like the diffusion variables. Given the centrality of these variables to the SDE definition, this inconsistency makes the mapping between the 'average voice' prior and the diffusion SDEs technically underspecified.

comments.title: Confusing definition of solver parameters
comments.quote: Consider the following class of fixed step size h reverse SDE solvers parameterized with triplets of real numbers { (ˆ κt,h, ˆ ωt,h, ˆ σt,h) |t = h, 2 h, .., 1 }:
comments.explanation: The notation { (ˆ κt,h, ˆ ωt,h, ˆ σt,h) |t = h, 2 h, .., 1 } introduces the triplets as a set indexed by t, but there is no explicit equation provided in the text defining the solver transformation using these parameters. While the general structure of a fixed-step SDE solver is standard (a linear combination of variables and noise), the paper lacks the formal equation (e.g., X_{t-h} = ...) that maps these specific parameters to the next step. I checked if it appears later—in Section 4 before Table 2, an equation appears (which was likely mangled by OCR as 'ˆ where ˆ t = 1, 1 − h, .., h ...'), but it never explicitly links the parameters κ, ω, and σ to the specific form of the update step.

comments.title: Missing definition of the reverse SDE solver update rule
comments.quote:
As for sampling, we considered the following class of reverse SDE solvers:

ˆ whereˆ t = 1, 1 − h, .., h and ξt are i.i.d. samples from N (0, I).
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction clearly failed to capture the actual formula for the solver update rule, leaving only the word 'where' followed by an incomplete index definition. Since this formula is central to the claimed 'maximum likelihood SDE solver' and the core contribution of the paper (reducing iterations), the absence of the explicit update equation makes the method underspecified and impossible to reproduce without referring to external sources or guessing the intended linear structure.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency for Wiener processes
comments.quote: where [−→] W is a forward Wiener process [...] and [←−] W is a backward Wiener process
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the reverse SDE is defined using d←W_t. In Section 3, Equations 6-F and 6-R, the text introduces the symbols [−→] W and [←−] W. While the notation is consistent within the paper, it is non-standard. The standard notation for backward Wiener processes in diffusion literature is usually dW_t (with a negative time direction) or dŵ_t. While the authors define their notation, the use of directional accents on the symbol W alongside directional arrows in the text/equations creates potential for confusion with standard Ito calculus notation where dW_t is inherently associated with the direction of the SDE.

comments.title: Possible Typographical Error in Reverse SDE Solver Formula
comments.quote: Equation (11): \hat{X}_{t-h} = \hat{X}_t + \hat{\kappa}_{t,h} \hat{X}_t + \hat{\omega}_{t,h} s_\theta(\hat{X}_t, t) + \hat{\sigma}_{t,h} \xi_t
comments.explanation: In most literature on reverse SDE solvers for DPMs (e.g., Song et al., 2021c), the reverse step for state advancement is typically written as \hat{X}_{t-h} = \dots (moving from t back to t-h). However, looking at the standard Euler-Maruyama discretization for the reverse SDE (2) provided in Section 2.2, which is $dX_t = [-\frac{1}{2}\beta_t X_t - \beta_t s_\theta(X_t, t)] dt + \sqrt{\beta_t} d\overleftarrow{W}_t$, a single small step backwards should physically correspond to an update where the drifts are negative relative to the forward direction. In Eq (11), the authors define the solver to produce the next state $\hat{X}_{t-h}$ from $\hat{X}_t$. While the notation $\hat{X}_{t-h} = \hat{X}_t + \dots$ is mathematically defined by the triple $(\hat{\kappa}, \hat{\omega}, \hat{\sigma})$, the drift terms $\hat{\kappa}$ and $\hat{\omega}$ act as the multiplier for $\hat{X}_t$ and $s_\theta$. The concern is whether the signs of $\hat{\kappa}^*$ and $\hat{\omega}^*$ derived in Theorem 1 implicitly account for the direction change. If the indices or signs are not explicitly reconciled with the SDE (2) drift coefficients (which involve $-\frac{1}{2}\beta_t X_t$), a reader might misinterpret the update direction. It is likely correct by design, but the ambiguity between the standard EM form $X_{rev} = X_t + f(X_t,t)h + g(X_t,t)dw$ and the specific signs required for a reverse SDE makes this definition potentially confusing without explicit sign conventions.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Probability Flow' definition
comments.quote: which we denote by $\hat{\omega}_{t,h} = 0, \hat{\sigma}_{t,h} = 0). \hat{PF-N} ( \hat{\kappa}_{t,h} = - 0 . 5, $
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the definition of the 'Probability Flow' (PF) sampling scheme includes an explicit value $\hat{\kappa}_{t,h} = -0.5$. However, the preamble 'which we denote by...' seems to have a parenthetical error or formatting overlap with the subsequent text '$\hat{\omega}_{t,h} = 0, \hat{\sigma}_{t,h} = 0)$'. This creates ambiguity: is the PF-N solver defined solely by $\kappa=-0.5, \omega=0, \sigma=0$? The snippet appears to be a fragmented equation description. While a reader familiar with Song et al. (2021c) can infer the intent, the text as written is structurally broken and unclear.

comments.title: Underspecified 'tau' parameter in Maximum Likelihood Solver
comments.quote: Maximum Likelihood($\tau = 0.1$)
comments.explanation: In Table 4, the authors evaluate a 'Maximum Likelihood' solver with varying parameters labeled as $\tau$. However, looking back at the theoretical framework (Theorem 1, Equations 10 and 11), there is no mention of the parameter $\tau$. The text does not explain what this parameter represents (e.g., a temperature, a weighting hyperparameter for the score, or a truncation constant). While it is clearly a variance-scaling or step-size adjustment factor, failing to define this variable in the methodology section makes the results in Table 4 impossible to replicate independently.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## Reviewer Feedback

This paper addresses an important problem—one-shot many-to-many voice conversion—and proposes a technically sound approach that combines an autoencoder architecture with a diffusion probabilistic model (DPM) decoder, alongside a novel stochastic differential equation (SDE) solver for faster inference. The paper is generally well-structured and clearly written, with a logical progression from problem definition to methodology to experimental validation. The authors demonstrate competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods and provide both empirical and theoretical justification for their proposed sampling scheme. However, the paper's most significant issue is insufficient clarity and rigor in several key areas. First, the description of the "average voice" encoder training procedure is vague: it is unclear whether the phoneme-level averaging is done per phoneme class or per phoneme instance, and the use of a forced aligner from a different dataset (LibriTTS) raises questions about domain mismatch and alignment errors that are not addressed. Second, the claim that the proposed SDE solver generalizes "to all popular kinds of DPMs" is overly strong without a formal proof or comprehensive ablation across diverse model families (e.g., score-based vs. denoising diffusion probabilistic models). Third, the evaluation metrics for voice conversion quality (e.g., MOS, speaker similarity) are standard but the paper would benefit from more detailed statistical significance reporting and error bars. Finally, while the authors emphasize real-time applications, no actual latency or computational cost measurements are provided, making it difficult to assess the practical impact of the accelerated sampling scheme. Addressing these gaps would substantially strengthen an already promising contribution.

comments.title: Euler‑Maruyama solver parameters are incorrectly stated
comments.quote: As a particular case it includes Euler-Maruyama solver (κˆ_t,h ≡ 0, ωˆ_t,h ≡ 0, σˆ_t,h ≡ √(β_t h))
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the Euler-Maruyama discretization of the reverse SDE (6‑R) corresponds to setting κˆ=0 and ωˆ=0 in the solver class (11). However, applying Euler-Maruyama to the reverse SDE dX̂_t = (-½β_t X̂_t − β_t s_θ) dt + √β_t dW with backward step h yields X̂_{t−h} = (1 + ½β_t h) X̂_t + (β_t h) s_θ + √(β_t h) ξ. This gives non‑zero coefficients for both X̂_t and s_θ (κ = ½β_t h, ω = β_t h), which are not zero for any finite h. The statement that Euler‑Maruyama has κ=0, ω=0 is therefore incorrect. This misrepresents a standard baseline and undermines the claim that the proposed solver differs from Euler‑Maruyama only by infinitesimal amounts.

comments.title: Missing definition of the optimal solver parameters
comments.quote:
The solver proposed in the Theorem 1 is expressed in terms of the values defined as follows:

where n is data dimensionality, Var (X_0 | X_t) is the covariance matrix …
comments.explanation: The text introduces the parameters κ^*, ω^*, σ^* that appear in Theorem 1 but the displayed equation containing their definitions is absent (likely lost during OCR). The subsequent sentences refer to 'the parameters (10)' without showing (10). Without these definitions, the theorem statement is incomplete and the precise form of the optimal solver cannot be verified. While the equation presumably exists in the original paper, the omission in this extracted passage makes the exposition ambiguous and hinders evaluation of the mathematical claims.

comments.title: Underspecified noise term in ML-N sampling method description
comments.quote: In practice it is not trivial to estimate variance of the conditional distribution Law ( _X_ 0 _|Xt_ ), so we skipped this term in _σt,h∗_ assuming this variance to be rather small because of strong conditioning on _gt_ ( _Y_ ) and just used ˆ _σt,h_ = _σt−h,t_ calling this sampling method _ML-N_ ( _N_ = 1 _/h_ is the number of SDE solver steps).
comments.explanation: The authors state that they skip the data-dependent variance term from the optimal σ*_{t,h} and instead use "ˆσ_{t,h} = σ_{t−h,t}".  The notation σ_{t−h,t} is not defined anywhere in the provided context, and it does not match any previously introduced symbol (e.g., σ*_{t,h} from equation (10) or σ_{t,h} for Euler–Maruyama).  The surrounding text also compares with Euler–Maruyama (ˆσ = √(β_t h)) and probability flow (ˆσ = 0), so the reader cannot determine what value was actually used for the ML-N method.  An OCR misread is plausible (e.g., σ*_{t,h} might have been corrupted to σ_{t−h,t}), but without a clear definition, a reproduction attempt would be blocked.  The rest of the paper suggests that the variance term was omitted entirely, implying that ˆσ_{t,h} should be the same as for Euler–Maruyama or some other fixed expression, but the quoted text explicitly differentiates it.

comments.title: Inconsistent MOS degradation values with reported tables
comments.quote: use as few as _N_ = 6 iterations with acceptable quality degradation of MOS 0 _._ 2 and 0 _._ 1 in terms of naturalness and speaker similarity respectively while two other competing methods lead to much more significant quality degradation.
comments.explanation: The sentence claims that using only 6 steps degrades naturalness by 0.2 MOS and speaker similarity by 0.1 MOS. However, the tables in the summary (Tables 2 and 3) show smaller or different degradations when comparing ML-6 to ML-30: for Diff-VCTK, naturalness is unchanged (3.73 vs 3.73) and similarity drops by 0.10; for Diff-LibriTTS, naturalness drops by 0.12 and similarity by 0.15. None of these give a 0.2 naturalness drop. The numbers 0.2 and 0.1 do not match any comparison visible in the provided tables. Without additional context (the preceding sentence is missing), the reader cannot reconcile the claim with the data. This appears to be an error in the reported degradation values, either a misstatement or a typo.

comments.title: Overclaim about 'all known diffusion model types' benefiting from the solver
comments.quote: Moreover, experiments on the image generation task show that all known diffusion model types can benefit from the proposed SDE solver.
comments.explanation: The conclusion states that 'all known diffusion model types' benefit from the solver. The experiments in Section 4.3 evaluate only VP, sub-VP, and VE models (from Song et al. 2021c). While these are popular types, the phrase 'all known' is overly broad and could include newer or less common variants that were not tested. The claim is stronger than what the evidence supports. A more measured statement like 'several common diffusion model types' would be appropriate.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## Reviewer Feedback

This paper addresses an important problem—one-shot many-to-many voice conversion—and proposes a technically sound approach that combines an autoencoder architecture with a diffusion probabilistic model (DPM) decoder, alongside a novel stochastic differential equation (SDE) solver for faster inference. The paper is generally well-structured and clearly written, with a logical progression from problem definition to methodology to experimental validation. The authors demonstrate competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods and provide both empirical and theoretical justification for their proposed sampling scheme. However, the paper's most significant issue is insufficient clarity and rigor in several key areas. First, the description of the "average voice" encoder training procedure is vague: it is unclear whether the phoneme-level averaging is done per phoneme class or per phoneme instance, and the use of a forced aligner from a different dataset (LibriTTS) raises questions about domain mismatch and alignment errors that are not addressed. Second, the claim that the proposed SDE solver generalizes "to all popular kinds of DPMs" is overly strong without a formal proof or comprehensive ablation across diverse model families (e.g., score-based vs. denoising diffusion probabilistic models). Third, the evaluation metrics for voice conversion quality (e.g., MOS, speaker similarity) are standard but the paper would benefit from more detailed statistical significance reporting and error bars. Finally, while the authors emphasize real-time applications, no actual latency or computational cost measurements are provided, making it difficult to assess the practical impact of the accelerated sampling scheme. Addressing these gaps would substantially strengthen an already promising contribution.

comments.title: Euler‑Maruyama solver parameters are incorrectly stated
comments.quote: As a particular case it includes Euler-Maruyama solver (κˆ_t,h ≡ 0, ωˆ_t,h ≡ 0, σˆ_t,h ≡ √(β_t h))
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the Euler-Maruyama discretization of the reverse SDE (6‑R) corresponds to setting κˆ=0 and ωˆ=0 in the solver class (11). However, applying Euler-Maruyama to the reverse SDE dX̂_t = (-½β_t X̂_t − β_t s_θ) dt + √β_t dW with backward step h yields X̂_{t−h} = (1 + ½β_t h) X̂_t + (β_t h) s_θ + √(β_t h) ξ. This gives non‑zero coefficients for both X̂_t and s_θ (κ = ½β_t h, ω = β_t h), which are not zero for any finite h. The statement that Euler‑Maruyama has κ=0, ω=0 is therefore incorrect. This misrepresents a standard baseline and undermines the claim that the proposed solver differs from Euler‑Maruyama only by infinitesimal amounts.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript addresses the critical challenge of slow inference in diffusion-based voice conversion by proposing a generalized, fast sampling scheme theoretically linked to maximum likelihood estimation, resulting in a high-quality one-shot many-to-many solution. The writing is coherent and well-organized, effectively contextualizing the work within the landscape of disentangled autoencoder approaches and clearly articulating the motivation for a diffusion-based decoder. A significant strength lies in the empirical and theoretical demonstration of the sampler's broad applicability across different DPM types, which could have broad impact beyond the specific task of voice conversion. However, the paper's novelty appears predominantly centered on the sampling acceleration rather than the generative model itself, as the core architecture relies heavily on the data-dependent prior framework introduced in the authors' prior work, raising questions about the distinctiveness of the VC contribution relative to established diffusion-based baselines. Furthermore, to fully substantiate claims of superiority, the experimental validation must provide rigorous comparisons not only against SOTA VC models but also against contemporary fast sampling methods, ensuring that the proposed speedup yields Pareto-optimal gains in latency and audio quality without sacrificing the fidelity advantages typically associated with diffusion models.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in decoder conditioning signature
comments.quote: We condition the decoder sθ = sθ ( Xˆt, X, g¯t ( Y ) , t ) on some trainable function gt ( Y ) to provide it with information about the target speaker
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the authors define $\bar{X}$ as the encoder output (the 'average voice' mel-spectrogram) and $g_t(Y)$ as the speaker conditioning network. However, in the decoder conditioning formula, the variable $X$ is used instead of $\bar{X}$, and the conditioning function is written with a bar ($\bar{g}_t(Y)$). This contradicts the established notation and introduces ambiguity about whether the unbarred $X$ refers to the noisy input or a typo for the average voice prior. While the intent is clearly to pass the average voice and conditioning vector to the score network, the inconsistent notation could confuse readers or downstream implementers. This is likely an author typo or OCR artifact, but it remains a technical inconsistency in the formal definitions.

comments.title: Ambiguous and potentially contradictory similarity metrics in Table 1
comments.quote: Table 1: Input types for speaker conditioning gt ( Y ) compared in terms of speaker similarity. ... Diff-VCTK (whole) | Most similar: 23.6% | Least similar: 48.6%
comments.explanation: In standard one-shot VC speaker similarity tests (typically a 3-way pairwise ranking among source, target A, and target B), chance performance is ~33%. For the Diff-VCTK 'whole' configuration, the 'Most similar' score is 23.6% (below chance) while 'Least similar' is 48.6% (above chance). This pattern strongly suggests the model systematically produces voices more similar to the *incorrect* target speaker, which directly contradicts the paper's claims of superior quality and the bolded 'best' scores for other configurations (e.g., 46.7% for wodyn). The table caption and surrounding text do not clarify how 'Most similar' and 'Least similar' are computed or whether they refer to different evaluation subsets or pairing strategies. This ambiguity could mislead readers into misinterpreting the model's actual speaker conditioning capability and the validity of the reported best results.

comments.title: Missing mathematical definitions for optimal parameters and solver recurrence
comments.quote:
The solver proposed in the Theorem 1 is expressed in terms of the values defined as follows:

where _n_ is data dimensionality, Var ( _X_ 0 _|Xt_ ) is the covariance matrix of the conditional data distribution Law( _X_ 0 _|Xt_ ) (so, Tr(Var ( _X_ 0 _|Xt_ )) is the overall variance across all _n_ dimensions) and the expectation E _Xt_ [ _·_ ] is taken with respect to the unconditional noisy data distribution Law( _Xt_ ).
comments.explanation: The actual formulas defining the optimal triplets $(\hat{\kappa}_{t,h}^*, \hat{\omega}_{t,h}^*, \hat{\sigma}_{t,h}^*)$ and the solver recurrence (11) are completely absent from the text. This appears to be an OCR extraction artifact rather than an author oversight, as the surrounding prose explicitly references them and the structural flow strongly implies missing equations. However, without these definitions, Theorem 1 and the proposed sampling scheme are mathematically incomplete. A careful reader relying on this passage would be unable to reproduce or verify the method without consulting external sources.

comments.title: Ambiguous prior specification for generative procedure
comments.quote: The generative procedure of a VP DPM consists in solving the reverse SDE (6-R) backwards in time starting from _X_[ˆ] 1 _∼N_ (0 _,_ I).
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the VC model explicitly uses a data-dependent prior $\mathcal{N}(\overline{X}, I)$ for the Mean Reverting Variance Preserving (MR-VP) formulation. While Section 3 correctly notes that the VP analysis can be transformed via a constant shift to match MR-VP, stating that the generative procedure starts from $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$ without immediately clarifying that the actual VC implementation samples from $\mathcal{N}(\overline{X}, I)$ could mislead a practitioner. The text treats the VP setup as generic, but the application context strictly requires the shifted prior. This should be explicitly reconciled to prevent implementation errors.

comments.title: Contradiction between claimed computational cost and practical implementation
comments.quote: The Theorem 1 provides an improved DPM sampling scheme which comes at no additional computational cost compared to standard methods (except for data-dependent term in _σ[∗]_ as discussed in Section 4.3) and requires neither model re-training nor extensive search on noise schedule space.
comments.explanation: The authors claim the scheme incurs 'no additional computational cost' except for a data-dependent term, yet immediately state in Section 4 that this exact term is skipped in practice because 'it is not trivial to estimate variance... so we skipped this term'. This creates a logical tension: if the optimal term is omitted, the deployed solver does not match the theoretically optimal parameters from Theorem 1, and the 'no additional cost' claim only applies to the approximated version, not the theorem itself. The framing overstates the equivalence between the theoretical optimum and the deployed method.

comments.title: Non-standard asymptotic notation $\overline{o}(1)$
comments.explanation: The notation $\overline{o}(1)$ is used to describe terms vanishing as step size $h \to 0$. Standard numerical analysis and stochastic calculus literature universally uses $o(1)$. The overline is non-standard and likely an OCR artifact or a typographical choice. While its mathematical meaning is clear from context, deviating from established notation without justification can confuse readers and break consistency with standard SDE discretization theory conventions.

comments.title: Inconsistent hat notation for applied solver parameters
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the authors set the solver parameters to their optimal values. However, the hat notation is applied inconsistently: $\hat{\omega}_{t,h} = \omega_{t,h}^*$ retains the hat, while $\kappa_{t,h} = \kappa_{t,h}^*$ and $\sigma_{t,h} = \sigma_{t,h}^*$ drop it. In Theorem 1, the hat denotes the solver's configurable parameters and the asterisk denotes optimality. Maintaining uniform notation here would improve clarity and prevent confusion about which variables are being set to their optimal values during inference.

comments.title: Theoretical justification for dropping the conditional variance term is slightly imprecise
comments.quote: assuming this variance to be rather small because of strong conditioning on _gt_ ( _Y_ ) and just used ˆ _σt,h_ = _σt−h,t_
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 derives an optimal reverse SDE solver where σ*_t,h includes a term proportional to Tr(Var(X_0|X_t))/λ_t. The authors drop this term for tractability, which is a widely accepted and practical heuristic in diffusion modeling. However, the justification—that 'strong conditioning on gt(Y)' makes this variance small—slightly conflates two distinct concepts: the *training-time conditioning network* gt(Y) used to inject target speaker info, and the *forward diffusion conditional data variance* Var(X_0|X_t) that appears in the likelihood derivation. The conditioning network does not directly shrink the uncertainty of the clean data given a noisy observation under the forward process; rather, it helps the score network approximate the conditional score. The omission is sound in practice and aligns with standard DDPM/DPM implementations, but the theoretical phrasing could be tightened to avoid implying a direct causal link between the decoder's conditioning architecture and the forward process's data variance.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a solid and well-articulated approach to one-shot many-to-many voice conversion by framing the task as a conditional diffusion probabilistic model. The writing is clear and establishes a strong technical motivation, particularly in how the encoder's role is defined to disentangle linguistic content from speaker identity via the "average voice" technique. However, the primary significance of the work appears to lie in the proposed fast Maximum Likelihood sampling scheme designed to mitigate the computational cost of diffusion

comments.title: Contradictory claim regarding 'same performance' on VCTK naturalness
comments.quote: show the same performance on the VCTK test set in terms of speech naturalness the latter being slightly better in terms of speaker similarity
comments.explanation: The table data indicates that for the VCTK test set, Diff-LibriTTS-ML-30 achieved a speech naturalness score of 3.96, while BNE-PPG-VC achieved 3.95. While these values are extremely close (differing by only 0.01), they are not the same, and the proposed model (3.96) is actually superior on this metric. The text then refers to 'the latter being slightly better in terms of speaker similarity,' which is factually correct for the similarity column (BNE: 3.27 vs ML-30: 3.23), but the phrasing and antecedent of 'latter' refer back to the naturalness clause, creating a logical contradiction where the authors claim their model performs the same (and implies BNE might be better) while the numbers show ML-30 is slightly better. This likely stems from small evaluation variance in MOS scores, but the wording is inaccurate.

comments.title: Unusual Notation for Solver Diffusion Coefficient
comments.quote: we skipped this term in \sigma^*_{t,h} ... and just used \hat{\sigma}_{t,h} = \sigma_{t-h, t} calling this sampling method ML-N
comments.explanation: The text describes an approximation for the Maximum Likelihood solver's diffusion coefficient \sigma. The notation \sigma_{t-h, t} is highly unusual and likely a typographical error. Given the context of fixed-step solvers, this probably intended to refer to the diffusion coefficient of the current step (\sigma_t) or the previous step (\sigma_{t-h}). This ambiguity makes the description of the exact implementation concrete parameter difficult to interpret.

progressive_original__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a solid and well-articulated approach to one-shot many-to-many voice conversion by framing the task as a conditional diffusion probabilistic model. The writing is clear and establishes a strong technical motivation, particularly in how the encoder's role is defined to disentangle linguistic content from speaker identity via the "average voice" technique. However, the primary significance of the work appears to lie in the proposed fast Maximum Likelihood sampling scheme designed to mitigate the computational cost of diffusion

comments.title: Contradictory claim regarding 'same performance' on VCTK naturalness
comments.quote: show the same performance on the VCTK test set in terms of speech naturalness the latter being slightly better in terms of speaker similarity
comments.explanation: The table data indicates that for the VCTK test set, Diff-LibriTTS-ML-30 achieved a speech naturalness score of 3.96, while BNE-PPG-VC achieved 3.95. While these values are extremely close (differing by only 0.01), they are not the same, and the proposed model (3.96) is actually superior on this metric. The text then refers to 'the latter being slightly better in terms of speaker similarity,' which is factually correct for the similarity column (BNE: 3.27 vs ML-30: 3.23), but the phrasing and antecedent of 'latter' refer back to the naturalness clause, creating a logical contradiction where the authors claim their model performs the same (and implies BNE might be better) while the numbers show ML-30 is slightly better. This likely stems from small evaluation variance in MOS scores, but the wording is inaccurate.
